title: JFrog Learn — Home
body: Skip to content Your personal JFrog knowledge base · built bottom-up Everything you need to master Artifactory & Xray . A growing, self-paced learning hub. Start with the fundamentals, then dive into focused deep-dives on replication, distribution, automation, security, and platform operations. Every fact links to primary JFrog documentation. New topics get added here over time. Start with the fundamentals Search the knowledge base 9 topic pages 3 lab tracks 0 assumptions Start here 📦 Foundations Fundamentals The problem these tools solve, the core vocabulary, the 4 repository types, Xray scanning, policies & watches, and three hands-on lab tracks. The bedrock everything else builds on. ~30 min · concepts + labs Core concepts 🔁 Multi-site Replication & Federation Push/pull replication vs. federated repositories. How to keep artifacts in sync across data centers and JPDs, and when to choose each approach. Deep-dive ⬆️ Lifecycle Build promotion in practice Move a build through dev → staging → release without rebuilding. build-info, status changes, copy vs. move, and the CLI/REST commands that do it. Deep-dive + CLI Distribution 🎁 Software supply chain Release Bundles & Distribution Immutable, signed collections of artifacts. How Distribution ships them to edge nodes and air-gapped sites, with provenance you can verify. Deep-dive Automation & security 🔌 Integration Artifactory REST API Authentication, the most useful endpoints, and how to drive them from curl and the JFrog CLI. Automate repo creation, user management, and more. Reference + examples 🐸 Shift-left Frogbot & IDE integration The Git bot that scans pull requests with Xray and opens fix PRs automatically. Plus the IDE plugins that flag vulnerabilities as you code. How-to + YAML 🚦 CI/CD JFrog Pipelines The YAML-based CI/CD engine. Integrations, resources, steps, and pipeline sources — build a working pipeline from a "Hello World" example up. How-to + YAML 🔑 Security Access tokens & permissions The full identity model: users, groups, permission targets, and scoped access tokens. How to grant least-privilege access the right way. Deep-dive + CLI Platform operations ☸️ K8s Kubernetes / Helm setup Two angles: installing Artifactory itself on Kubernetes via the JFrog Helm charts, and using Artifactory as your Helm chart registry for helm pull / push . How-to + CLI How to use this site A knowledge base that grows with you This is built to be your single learning hub for JFrog. Read the Fundamentals first if you're new — it assumes nothing. Then jump into any deep-dive. Use / or ⌘K anywhere to search across every page. Whenever there's a new JFrog topic to learn, it gets added here as another page. Tip Every page links to primary JFrog documentation for the details. This site is the map and the mental models; the official docs are the full reference. A self-paced learning hub for JFrog Artifactory & Xray. All facts link to primary JFrog documentation and reputable sources, current as of June 2026. Product names belong to JFrog Ltd. esc ↑ ↓ navigate ↵ open / or ⌘K to search

title: Access tokens & permissions
body: Skip to content On this page Access tokens Token scopes Creating a token Users, groups, targets Permission targets Set up permissions Home / Access tokens & permissions Platform Security How-to Access tokens & permissions Authentication answers “who are you?”; authorization answers “what may you touch?” JFrog uses access tokens for the first and a Users → Groups → Permission Targets hierarchy for the second. Get this model right and everything else — the REST API, Frogbot, CI — falls into place. Concept Access tokens — the modern way to authenticate An access token is a signed credential that identifies you (or a service) to the JFrog Platform. Unlike a password, a token is scoped (it can be limited to specific resources), revocable (kill it without changing your password), and expirable (it can auto-die after a set time). That makes tokens the right choice for CI jobs, the REST API, Frogbot, and IDE plugins. JFrog — Access Tokens . No password exposure. A leaked token can be revoked in isolation; your account password is never embedded in scripts. Scoped to least privilege. Grant a token only the access a given job actually needs. Sent as a Bearer header. Every authenticated REST call uses Authorization: Bearer <token> (see the REST API page). Scopes Token scopes: user, admin, and custom Scope What it grants Use for User The same permissions the named user already has (inherits their group memberships and permission targets). A token that acts “as me” for everyday automation. Admin Full administrative access to the platform. Platform bootstrap/IaC only — guard it carefully. Custom / scoped Exactly the resource scopes you specify (e.g. read on one repo). Least-privilege service tokens for a single job. Default to least privilege Reach for a custom-scoped token tied to a service identity before an admin token. If a custom token leaks, the blast radius is one repo, not your whole platform. How-to Creating an access token — three ways 1. From the UI Open token management. Administration → User Management → Access Tokens → Generate Token . Choose the scope. Pick the user/identity, set an expiry, and (optionally) restrict the scope. Copy it once. The token value is shown a single time — store it in a secret manager immediately. 2. With the JFrog CLI Create a token (alias jf atc ) jf access-token-create --expiry=3600 # scope it to a specific user, or add --groups / --scope jf atc --groups=ci-bots --expiry=86400 3. Over the REST API POST to the tokens endpoint curl -XPOST -H "Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN_TOKEN" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ https://yourname.jfrog.io/access/api/v1/tokens \ -d '{ "subject": "ci-bot", "scope": "applied-permissions/groups:ci-bots", "expires_in": 86400 }' Sources: JFrog — Access Tokens , JFrog CLI — Access Tokens . Model The authorization model: Users → Groups → Permission Targets JFrog never grants permissions to a user directly on a repo. Instead, permissions flow through a three-layer hierarchy. Understanding it is the key to keeping access manageable as your platform grows. JFrog — Permissions . 1 Users people & service identities → belong to → 2 Groups roles, e.g. ci-bots, devs → assigned in → 3 Permission targets access to repos/builds Assign users to groups (by role), then grant groups access in permission targets . New hires just join a group and inherit the right access — no per-user wiring. JFrog — Permissions . Authorization Permission targets — fine-grained access A permission target binds a set of resources (repositories, builds, or release bundles) to the actions a user/group may perform on them. Actions include read, write/deploy, delete, manage, annotate (set properties), and cache management. You can target specific repos or use wildcards. Element Meaning Resource scope Which repos/builds/release-bundles this target covers. ANY Wildcard — every repository. ANY LOCAL Every local repository. ANY REMOTE Every remote repository. Actions read / deploy(write) / delete / manage / annotate / cache, granted per user or group. Read vs. deploy vs. manage Read = pull/resolve artifacts. Deploy = push new artifacts. Delete = remove them. Manage = edit the permission target itself (grant access to others) — the most powerful, hand it out sparingly. Sources: JFrog — Set up users, groups & permissions , JFrog — Permissions . How-to Wiring up a least-privilege setup Create role groups. Administration → User Management → Groups → add e.g. developers , ci-bots , release-managers . Add users to groups. Put each person/service identity in the group(s) matching their role. Create permission targets. Administration → User Management → Permissions → add a target; pick the repos (or ANY LOCAL ) and grant each group only the actions it needs. Mint scoped tokens. For automation, create custom-scoped tokens bound to the ci-bots group rather than handing out personal tokens. Review & rotate. Periodically audit targets, expire stale tokens, and confirm no group has more than it needs. Tie it together This model powers everything else on this site: the token you mint here is the Bearer in the REST API page, the JF_ACCESS_TOKEN for Frogbot , and the credential a Pipelines integration stores. ← Previous JFrog Pipelines Next → Kubernetes / Helm setup All facts link to primary JFrog documentation, current as of June 2026. title: Build promotion in practice
body: Skip to content On this page What promotion is build-info & identity Copy vs. move Promote via CLI Promote via REST Repo layout pattern Home / Build promotion Artifactory Lifecycle CLI + REST Build promotion in practice Promotion is how a binary graduates from dev to staging to release without ever being rebuilt — so what you tested is byte-for-byte what ships. It hinges on one idea: build-info that tracks artifacts by checksum, wherever they move. Concept What build promotion actually is Build promotion changes a build's status to a more mature stage — for example dev → staging → released — and can optionally move or copy the build's artifacts (and their dependencies) into a target repository and set properties on them. The crucial part: no rebuild happens . The same binary that passed your tests is the one that advances. JFrog — How build promotion works . 🎟️ One-sentence analogy Promotion is a stamp on a passport , not a new passport. The traveller (your binary) is unchanged; it just earns a visa for the next country (stage). Because it's the same passport, you have proof the thing entering production is exactly the thing you vetted. Foundation build-info and artifact identity Promotion only works because Artifactory records build-info — metadata capturing which artifacts a build produced, which dependencies it consumed, and the environment it ran in. Artifacts are tracked by their checksum (SHA-1/SHA-256) plus the build.name and build.number properties, so Artifactory knows an artifact's identity no matter what repository it currently sits in. JFrog — Manage builds , Build integration . Location-agnostic. Because identity is the checksum + build coordinates, moving an artifact between repos doesn't lose its lineage. Full traceability. From a release artifact you can walk back to the exact build, its dependencies, and its source commit. Required first step. Your CI must publish build-info (the JFrog CLI does this automatically on jf rt build-publish ) before you can promote that build. Choice Copy vs. move — and properties Option Effect When to use --copy=true Duplicates artifacts into the target repo; originals stay in the source. You want the artifact in both the staging and release repos (common). --copy=false (move) Relocates artifacts to the target; removes them from the source. Strict single-home layouts where each stage owns its artifacts exclusively. Set properties Stamps key/value properties on promoted artifacts (e.g. stage=released ). Driving downstream queries, watches, or cleanup policies. Default to copy Copying keeps a clear audit trail at each stage and avoids surprises if a later gate fails. Use move only when you deliberately want a single physical home per maturity level. CLI Promote a build with the JFrog CLI The command is jf rt build-promote (alias jf rt bpr ). You name the build, its number, and the target repo, plus options for copy/move and the new status. JFrog — Build promotion . Promote build "TestBuild" #49 to a release repo jf rt build-promote \ --status=Released \ --copy=true \ --source-repo=staging-local \ TestBuild 49 release-local This copies build TestBuild/49 's artifacts from staging-local into release-local and marks the build status Released . Drop --copy=true (or set it false) to move instead. Prerequisite The build must already be published to Artifactory: jf rt build-publish TestBuild 49 after collecting build-info during your CI run. Only then is there a build to promote. REST Promote a build via the REST API CI systems that don't use the CLI can call the promotion endpoint directly. JFrog — Build Promotion REST API . POST to the build-promote endpoint POST /artifactory/api/build/promote/TestBuild/49 Authorization: Bearer <access-token> Content-Type: application/json { "status": "Released", "sourceRepo": "staging-local", "targetRepo": "release-local", "copy": true, "properties": { "stage": ["released"] } } The path carries the build name and number; the JSON body carries everything else. See the Artifactory REST API page for auth details and the jf api shortcut. Pattern The repository layout promotion assumes 1 dev-local CI publishes here → promote → 2 staging-local passes tests + Xray → promote → 3 release-local production-approved Each arrow is a build-promote that advances status and (optionally) copies the same binary forward. An Xray Watch on each repo can gate the move. JFrog onboarding best practices . Tie it back to Xray The power move: a Policy + Watch on staging-local blocks promotion to release-local if a High/Critical CVE with an available fix is present. Promotion becomes your enforced quality gate, not a manual courtesy. ← Previous Replication & Federation Next → Release Bundles & Distribution All facts link to primary JFrog documentation, current as of June 2026. title: Frogbot & IDE integration
body: Skip to content On this page What Frogbot is PR scan vs. repo scan Setup on GitHub Actions The workflow YAML IDE integration Home / Frogbot & IDE Xray Shift-left How-to Frogbot & IDE integration Shift security as far left as it goes: Frogbot scans every pull request with Xray and can open fix PRs on its own, while the JFrog IDE plugins flag vulnerable dependencies while you're still typing. Concept What Frogbot is Frogbot is a Git bot that scans your pull requests and repositories for security vulnerabilities using JFrog Xray. When a developer opens a PR, Frogbot scans it and posts the results as a comment (a "decoration") directly on the PR — and if it finds no new issues, it says so. It works with GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and Azure Repos. jfrog/frogbot . Only flags new issues. A PR scan compares the project state before and after the change, so it reports only vulnerabilities the PR introduces — not pre-existing noise. Opens fix PRs. When fixable issues are found and auto-fix is enabled, Frogbot opens a pull request that upgrades the vulnerable dependency to a fixed version. Requires Xray 3.29.0+. Frogbot uses your JFrog platform's Xray to do the actual scanning. JFrog — Commit & PR scan . Two modes Pull-request scan vs. repository scan Mode Command What it does Pull Request frogbot scan-pull-request Scans a PR by diffing before/after the change. Only new issues are reported, as PR comments. Repository (commit) scan frogbot scan-repository Scans the latest commit of a branch for all existing vulnerabilities. With auto-fix on, opens fix PRs for upgradable dependencies. Source: JFrog — Scanning Commits and Pull Requests . Required environment variables Both modes need JF_URL (your platform URL, e.g. https://my.jfrog.io ) and JF_ACCESS_TOKEN (a scoped token), plus a Git token ( JF_GIT_TOKEN / the CI's built-in token) so Frogbot can comment and open PRs. How-to Setting up Frogbot on GitHub Actions Allow Frogbot to open PRs. In GitHub → Settings → Actions → General , check "Allow GitHub Actions to create and approve pull requests." (Recommended for OSS) Create a frogbot environment. Settings → Environments → New environment named frogbot , and add reviewers. Those reviewers must approve before Frogbot scans a PR — a safety gate for public repos. Add the workflow file. Drop a Frogbot workflow into .github/workflows/ (template below), or use the JFrog GitHub App to have it opened for you automatically across selected repos. Add secrets/vars. Provide JF_URL and JF_ACCESS_TOKEN as repository secrets. The built-in GITHUB_TOKEN covers Git operations. Sources: JFrog — Frogbot GitHub Actions install , JFrog GitHub App bulk install . YAML The PR-scan workflow A minimal GitHub Actions workflow that runs Frogbot on every pull request. JFrog — GitHub PR scans . name: "Frogbot Scan Pull Request" on: pull_request_target: types: [ opened, synchronize ] permissions: pull-requests: write contents: read id-token: write jobs: scan-pull-request: runs-on: ubuntu-latest # PR must be approved by a "frogbot" environment reviewer before scanning environment: frogbot steps: - uses: jfrog/frogbot@v2 env: JF_URL: ${{ vars.JF_URL }} JF_ACCESS_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.JF_ACCESS_TOKEN }} JF_GIT_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} For the repository (auto-fix) mode, use a separate workflow triggered on push to your default branch (or on a schedule) that runs Frogbot in scan-repository mode. Configure Frogbot in GitHub Actions . Shift-left IDE integration — catch it before the PR Even earlier than the PR is the editor. JFrog ships plugins for VS Code, IntelliJ/JetBrains IDEs, Visual Studio, and Eclipse that scan your project's dependencies against Xray and surface vulnerabilities inline as you work. JFrog — IDE integrations . 🧩 Inline findings Vulnerable dependencies are highlighted in your dependency files with severity and details, without leaving the editor. 🔗 Connect to your JPD Point the plugin at your JFrog platform URL + an access token; it uses your Xray for the database and policies. ⏪ Earliest feedback Fix issues before you even commit — the cheapest place to fix anything is in the editor. The shift-left ladder IDE plugin (as you type) → Frogbot (on the PR) → Xray indexing + Watch (in Artifactory) → build-scan gate (in CI). Each rung catches issues later and more expensively, so push detection as far left as you can. ← Previous Artifactory REST API Next → JFrog Pipelines All facts link to primary JFrog documentation, current as of June 2026. new issues. a pr scan compares the project state before and after the change, so it reports only vulnerabilities the pr introduces — not pre-existing noise. opens fix prs. when fixable issues are found and auto-fix is enabled, frogbot opens a pull request that upgrades the vulnerable dependency to a fixed version. requires xray 3.29.0+. frogbot uses your jfrog platform's xray to do the actual scanning. jfrog — commit & pr scan . two modes pull-request scan vs. repository scan mode command what it does pull request frogbot scan-pull-request scans a pr by diffing before/after the change. only new issues are reported, as pr comments. repository (commit) scan frogbot scan-repository scans the latest commit of a branch for all existing vulnerabilities. with auto-fix on, opens fix prs for upgradable dependencies. source: jfrog — scanning commits and pull requests . required environment variables both modes need jf_url (your platform url, e.g. https://my.jfrog.io ) and jf_access_token (a scoped token), plus a git token ( jf_git_token / the ci's built-in token) so frogbot can comment and open prs. how-to setting up frogbot on github actions allow frogbot to open prs. in github → settings → actions → general , check "allow github actions to create and approve pull requests." (recommended for oss) create a frogbot environment. settings → environments → new environment named frogbot , and add reviewers. those reviewers must approve before frogbot scans a pr — a safety gate for public repos. add the workflow file. drop a frogbot workflow into .github/workflows/ (template below), or use the jfrog github app to have it opened for you automatically across selected repos. add secrets/vars. provide jf_url and jf_access_token as repository secrets. the built-in github_token covers git operations. sources: jfrog — frogbot github actions install , jfrog github app bulk install . yaml the pr-scan workflow a minimal github actions workflow that runs frogbot on every pull request. jfrog — github pr scans . name: "frogbot scan pull request" on: pull_request_target: types: [ opened, synchronize ] permissions: pull-requests: write contents: read id-token: write jobs: scan-pull-request: runs-on: ubuntu-latest # pr must be approved by a "frogbot" environment reviewer before scanning environment: frogbot steps: - uses: jfrog/frogbot@v2 env: jf_url: ${{ vars.jf_url }} jf_access_token: ${{ secrets.jf_access_token }} jf_git_token: ${{ secrets.github_token }} for the repository (auto-fix) mode, use a separate workflow triggered on push to your default branch (or on a schedule) that runs frogbot in scan-repository mode. configure frogbot in github actions . shift-left ide integration — catch it before the pr even earlier than the pr is the editor. jfrog ships plugins for vs code, intellij/jetbrains ides, visual studio, and eclipse that scan your project's dependencies against xray and surface vulnerabilities inline as you work. jfrog — ide integrations . 🧩 inline findings vulnerable dependencies are highlighted in your dependency files with severity and details, without leaving the editor. 🔗 connect to your jpd point the plugin at your jfrog platform url + an access token; it uses your xray for the database and policies. ⏪ earliest feedback fix issues before you even commit — the cheapest place to fix anything is in the editor. the shift-left ladder ide plugin (as you type) → frogbot (on the pr) → xray indexing + watch (in artifactory) → build-scan gate (in ci). each rung catches issues later and more expensively, so push detection as far left as you can. ← previous artifactory rest api next → jfrog pipelines all facts link to primary jfrog documentation, current as of june 2026. product names belong to jfrog ltd. esc ↑ ↓ navigate ↵ open / or ⌘k to search

title: Artifactory & Xray from first principles
body: Skip to content On this page The problem it solves Vocabulary first What Artifactory is The 4 repository types How it fits together Package types What Xray is How scanning works Policies & Watches Choose your track CLI cheat sheet Full mental model Home / Fundamentals Artifactory Xray Beginner → core Artifactory & Xray from first principles Two products, one job: control every binary your software is built from, and prove it's safe. This page builds the whole picture from the bottom up — plain-language concepts, diagrams, comparison tables, then real hands-on labs. Foundations The problem these tools solve Before any product names, understand the pain. When you build software, your code is only a small part of what actually ships. The rest is binaries — compiled libraries, container images, packages — that you either build yourself or download from the internet. What is a "binary" / "artifact"? Anything your build produces or consumes that isn't source code: a .jar , an npm package, a Docker image, a Python wheel, a Helm chart, a compiled .exe . The generic industry word is artifact . JFrog calls itself an "artifact repository manager" — a place to store and serve these. Without a central system, teams hit four recurring problems: 01 Where do builds get dependencies? Everyone pulls directly from the public internet (npm registry, Docker Hub, Maven Central). It's slow, breaks when those sites are down, and you have no control over what enters your builds. 02 Where do our builds go? You compile something internally. Where does it live so other teams and your CI/CD can reliably grab the exact version? Email and shared drives don't scale. 03 Is any of it dangerous? A public package might carry a known vulnerability (a CVE ) or a banned open-source license. You need to know before it reaches production — not after a breach. 04 Can we prove what's inside? Auditors and customers increasingly ask: "List every component in this release." That list is called an SBOM . Producing it by hand is impossible. Artifactory solves problems 1, 2 & 4 — it's the single, trusted home for all binaries flowing in and out. Xray solves problem 3 (and helps with 4) — it continuously scans everything Artifactory stores for vulnerabilities and license risks. They are designed to work together: Artifactory is the warehouse, Xray is the security inspector standing inside it. JFrog — What is Artifactory . Foundations Vocabulary you'll see everywhere JFrog docs assume you know these. Skim now, refer back later. Artifact / Binary Any built/downloaded file: a package, image, library. The thing being stored. Repository A named container inside Artifactory that holds artifacts of one package type (e.g. a Docker repo, an npm repo). Package type The ecosystem format — Docker, Maven, npm, PyPI, Helm, etc. Artifactory "speaks" each one natively. Registry The package-ecosystem word for a repository (e.g. "Docker registry"). Same idea. Dependency A third-party artifact your code needs to build or run. CVE Common Vulnerabilities & Exposures — a globally unique ID for a known security flaw, e.g. CVE-2021-44228 (Log4Shell). SCA Software Composition Analysis — scanning the open-source pieces of your software for risk. This is Xray's core job. SBOM Software Bill of Materials — a complete, machine-readable inventory of every component in a release (formats: SPDX, CycloneDX). JPD JFrog Platform Deployment — one running instance of the JFrog platform (your server / cloud tenant). build-info Metadata Artifactory records about a build: which artifacts, which dependencies, which environment. Enables traceability. Promotion Moving an artifact from one repo to the next as it passes quality gates (dev → staging → release). Watch + Policy Xray's enforcement pair: a Policy defines the rules, a Watch says where to apply them. (Detailed later.) Artifactory What Artifactory actually is JFrog Artifactory is a universal binary repository manager — one central place to store, version, secure and serve every artifact your organization produces or depends on. "Universal" means it natively supports 40+ package formats (Docker, Maven, npm, PyPI, Helm, NuGet, Go, Terraform, even ML models) at the same time. JFrog Artifactory . 📦 One-sentence analogy Artifactory is a smart warehouse for software parts . Parts arrive from outside suppliers (the public internet), parts are manufactured in-house (your builds), and everything is catalogued, version-stamped, access-controlled, and handed out through a single front desk. Three things make it more than a file server: It speaks every package language. Your npm install , docker pull , or mvn build point at Artifactory and it behaves exactly like the native registry would. It proxies and caches the internet. Public dependencies pass through Artifactory once, get cached, and stay available even if the upstream source goes down. Stack Overflow explanation . It's a control point. Because everything flows through it, you can apply access control, scanning (Xray), and policies in one place. JFrog overview . Artifactory The 4 repository types (the core concept) If you learn only one thing about Artifactory, learn this. Every repository is one of four types. They combine into a clean pattern that every JFrog setup follows. Outside world Public registries Docker Hub · npm · Maven Central · PyPI proxy & cache → Inside Artifactory REMOTE repo caches external artifacts LOCAL repo stores your own builds ▸ both wrapped by ▸ VIRTUAL repo one URL = local + remote combined single URL → Developer / CI your build tool npm · docker · mvn · pip The standard pattern: developers and CI talk to one virtual repo URL ; Artifactory routes deploys to the local repo and resolves dependencies from local first, then the remote cache, then the internet. Federated repos (4th type) sit alongside for multi-site sync. JFrog Repository Management . Type What it does Can you upload to it? Real-world use Local Physically stores artifacts your org creates internally. Yes — this is your deploy target. Your CI pushes built Docker images, release JARs, internal libraries here. Remote A caching proxy for an external registry. Fetches on demand, caches locally. No — artifacts arrive by proxying. A proxy of Docker Hub or npm registry so builds are fast and survive outages. Virtual Aggregates many local + remote repos under one URL . Resolves in priority order. Via default deploy target (points to a local). The single endpoint your developers configure. Hides internal/external complexity. Federated A local repo auto-mirrored bi-directionally across multiple sites (JPDs). Yes — like a local, but synced. Global teams (London ↔ Tokyo) sharing one source of truth. Enterprise tier. Sources: JFrog config entities , JFrog Federated Repositories . Local/remote/virtual need a Pro subscription; federated needs Enterprise X+. The "aha" moment A virtual repo is the magic layer. Your npm client points at one URL. Behind it, Artifactory checks your local repo first (internal packages), then the remote cache (previously downloaded public packages), and only then reaches out to the public internet. The developer never has to know or care where a package lives. JFrog artifact management demo . Want to go deeper on keeping repos in sync across sites? See Replication & Federation . Artifactory How it fits in your pipeline Zoom out. Here's where Artifactory sits in a normal CI/CD flow, and where Xray plugs in. This is the picture to hold in your head. 1 Developer writes code, runs build locally → 2 CI/CD GitHub Actions builds artifact → 3 Artifactory stores artifact + records build-info ↓ every artifact is indexed & scanned 4 Xray scans for CVEs & license risk → 5 Policy gate pass → promote · fail → block/alert → 6 Production only approved artifacts ship Artifacts get promoted through repositories as they pass each gate. Xray watches the whole warehouse continuously, so a CVE disclosed tomorrow still flags an artifact you stored last month. JFrog DevSecOps best practices . What's "promotion"? You typically have separate repos per maturity stage: dev-local → staging-local → release-local . Promotion = moving (or copying) an artifact up a stage once it passes tests and scans. Same binary, no rebuild — so what you tested is exactly what ships. Full walkthrough on the Build promotion page. Artifactory Package types it supports "Universal" is the selling point: 40+ ecosystems, one platform. You don't need them all — pick the ones your stack uses. Here are the common ones a DevOps team touches. Full list . Docker / OCI Helm Maven Gradle npm PyPI Go NuGet Cargo (Rust) Conan (C++) Debian RPM / YUM Terraform Conda Hugging Face (ML) RubyGems Generic (anything) DevOps reality check For a typical cloud-native team, you'll create repos for: Docker (your images), Helm (your charts), and a remote proxy of Docker Hub . Add npm / PyPI / Go remotes per language. The Generic type stores literally anything (tarballs, configs, ML weights) when no native type fits. Xray What Xray actually is JFrog Xray is a Software Composition Analysis (SCA) tool. It scans everything Artifactory stores — packages, container images, builds — to detect security vulnerabilities, malicious packages, license risks, and operational issues. It's natively integrated with Artifactory, so binary scanning turns on with a single checkbox. JFrog Xray docs . 🔍 One-sentence analogy If Artifactory is the warehouse, Xray is the security inspector living inside it — X-raying every box that arrives, cross-checking it against a global database of known threats, and raising an alarm (or locking the door) when something dangerous is found. 🛡️ Vulnerabilities Matches components against CVE databases (NVD, GitHub, vendor feeds + JFrog's own security research) to find known flaws. ⚖️ License compliance Flags open-source licenses your org bans or must track (e.g. GPL in a proprietary product). 🐛 Malicious packages Detects deliberately poisoned packages — including malicious ML models on Hugging Face. 📋 SBOM & impact Generates a full Software Bill of Materials and shows the impact graph of any flaw across your components. Sources: Xray architecture overview , JFrog SBOM . Xray How scanning actually works Xray's superpower is deep recursive scanning . It doesn't just look at your top-level package — it peels open every layer: dependencies, dependencies-of-dependencies, files inside Docker layers, archives inside archives. JFrog Impact Analysis . Deep recursive scan — peeling the onion Your Docker image Base OS layer App package (npm/jar) Direct dependency ↳ vulnerable lib · CVE-2021-44228 Xray finds the vulnerable component buried 5 levels deep, then uses impact analysis to show every artifact, build, and image affected by it. JFrog vulnerability detection . The scanning lifecycle Index. When a repo is marked for Xray, every artifact is broken into its component graph and indexed. Scan. Each component is matched against the vulnerability + license databases. Continuous re-check. When a new CVE is published, Xray re-evaluates already-stored artifacts — no rescan needed. This is why it catches yesterday's "safe" image today. Report & enforce. Findings appear as violations (when they break a policy) with severity, affected components, and remediation (which version fixes it). Understanding scan results . Where you can trigger a scan How Best for Inside Artifactory (automatic) Enable Xray indexing on a repo — scans on upload Continuous coverage of everything stored CI/CD pipeline jf build-scan / jf scan Failing a build before bad code ships Local machine (shift-left) jf scan on source, jf docker scan on an image Catching issues before you even commit IDE / Pull request JFrog IDE plugins & Frogbot Instant developer feedback + autofix PRs Source: JFrog CLI command summaries . For PR scanning and IDE setup, see Frogbot & IDE integration . Xray Policies & Watches (how enforcement works) Finding problems is useless unless you act on them. Xray's enforcement runs on two linked objects. This trips up newcomers, so here's the clean split: Defines the RULES Policy A set of rules describing what counts as a problem and what to do about it. Type: Security · License · Operational risk Rule: e.g. "severity ≥ High" or "license = GPL" Action: notify · fail build · block download · create ticket applied via Defines the SCOPE Watch A binding that says where a policy applies and turns it on. Targets: specific repos, builds, or projects Attaches: one or more policies Result: matches → violations get generated Remember it this way Policy = the law (what's illegal + the punishment). Watch = the jurisdiction + the police (where the law applies and who enforces it). A finding only becomes a violation when a Watch points a Policy at the repo where it lives. JFrog Policy & Governance . Severity levels you'll triage Critical High Medium Low Unknown A common starter policy: "generate violations for High + Critical security issues, but only when a fixed version is available" — so you're not alerted on problems you can't yet fix. Stack Overflow on fix-version rule . Hands-on Choose your hands-on track Reading gets you understanding; doing gets you skill. Pick a track based on how deep you want to go. All three reach the same goal: store an artifact, then scan it with Xray. ☁️ JFrog Cloud (free tier) 🐳 Local Docker (self-hosted) ⌨️ Concepts + CLI Fastest path to a working platform. JFrog runs a free cloud tier (AWS/Azure/GCP) — no install, ready in minutes. Best first stop. Start free . 1 Sign up for the free cloud subscription. Go to jfrog.com/start-free → choose Cloud (free). You get a tenant like yourname.jfrog.io with Artifactory + Xray. Free tier: ~2 GB storage, 10 GB transfer. Free tier announcement . 2 Log in to the web UI. Explore the two main tabs: Application (browse repos/artifacts) and Administration (create repos, configure Xray). 3 Create your first repositories. Administration → Repositories → + Add Repository . Make a Local Docker repo ( docker-local ), a Remote proxy of Docker Hub ( docker-remote ), and a Virtual repo combining both ( docker ). Repo creation walkthrough . 4 Connect the CLI. Install JFrog CLI (see the CLI tab) then run jf c add and paste your *.jfrog.io URL + a token. Verify with jf rt ping . 5 Push an image. jf docker push yourname.jfrog.io/docker-local/demo:1.0 — then find it in the UI under your local repo. 6 Turn on Xray. Administration → Xray → Indexed Resources → add docker-local . The image scans automatically. 7 Create a Policy + Watch. Administration → Xray → Watches & Policies . Make a Security policy (rule: severity ≥ High, action: notify) and a Watch targeting docker-local . Policies/watches video . 8 Read the results. Open the artifact → Xray tab. Review vulnerabilities by severity, affected components, and fix versions. You've completed the loop. 🎉 Why start here Zero infrastructure to maintain, real cloud UI, and everything in this guide works out of the box. Graduate to local Docker once you want to understand the moving parts. Run the whole platform on your Mac (OrbStack/Docker). You'll see exactly what services make up Artifactory + Xray. Requires Docker Engine 25+ & Docker Compose v2; ARM64 (Apple Silicon) is supported via Docker. Install guide . Heads up on resources The full platform (Artifactory + Xray + PostgreSQL + RabbitMQ) wants ~4 CPU / 8 GB RAM. Your 8 GB Mac setup is the realistic minimum — close other heavy apps. 1 Download the trial Docker-Compose bundle. curl -L "https://releases.jfrog.io/artifactory/jfrog-prox/org/artifactory/pro/docker/jfrog-platform-trial-prox/[RELEASE]/jfrog-platform-trial-prox-[RELEASE]-compose.tar.gz" -o jfrog-compose.tar.gz Source 2 Extract & enter the directory. tar -xvf jfrog-compose.tar.gz cd jfrog-platform-trial-prox-* 3 Run the config script (sets up folder structure + the .env file Compose uses). Follow its prompts. 4 Start everything. docker compose -p trial-pro up -d Give it a few minutes — Xray boots after Artifactory and PostgreSQL are healthy. Xray install steps . 5 Open the UI. Browse to http://localhost:8082 . Default login admin / password — change it immediately. 6 Apply your free license. Paste the license key from your JFrog account to unlock Xray. 7 From here, follow Cloud steps 3–8 — create local/remote/virtual repos, push an image, enable Xray indexing, set a policy + watch, read results. The UI is identical. 8 Tear down cleanly. docker compose -p trial-pro down Add -v only if you want to wipe the data volumes too. Prefer Kubernetes? See Kubernetes / Helm setup for installing Artifactory on a cluster with the JFrog Helm charts. For when you don't want to run a server — just learn the commands & integration patterns. The JFrog CLI binary is jf . One tool drives Artifactory and Xray. CLI summaries . 1 Install the CLI (macOS, Homebrew): brew install jfrog-cli Or one-liner: curl -fL https://install-cli.jfrog.io | sh . Verify: jf --version . 2 Configure a server connection. jf c add my-jpd --url=https://yourname.jfrog.io --interactive Use an access token, not a password. Test: jf rt ping . 3 Scan your source code (shift-left) — no upload needed: jf scan . Scans the dependencies in the current project against Xray. Source-scan docs . 4 Scan a local Docker image: jf docker scan my-app:1.0.0 First run downloads ~100 MB of Xray scanner binaries. jf docker docs . 5 Scan an artifact already in Artifactory, against a watch: jf xr scan --target "docker-local/demo:1.0" --watches "my-watch" Xray CLI commands . 6 Wire it into GitHub Actions (fail the build on Critical issues): - name: Security Scan run: jf build-scan ${{ github.repository }} ${{ github.run_number }} --fail=true --vuln=Critical CI integration . Note: the build must be added under Administration → Xray → Indexed Resources first. Mental model for the CLI jf rt … = Artifactory (uploads, downloads, repos). jf xr … = Xray (scans). jf scan / jf docker scan / jf build-scan = convenient shortcuts that combine both. Hands-on CLI cheat sheet The commands you'll reach for most. jf is the binary; older docs use jfrog . Reference . Command What it does jf c add Add a JFrog server connection jf rt ping Test the Artifactory connection jf rt u <file> <repo> Upload (deploy) an artifact jf rt dl <repo/path> Download an artifact jf docker push <image> Push a Docker image (auto-login + build-info) jf scan . Scan local source dependencies with Xray jf docker scan <image> Scan a local Docker image jf xr scan --target … --watches … Scan an artifact in Artifactory against a watch jf build-scan <name> <num> --fail=true Scan a build in CI; fail on violations Synthesis The full mental model on one screen Everything above, compressed. If you can re-draw this from memory, you understand the platform. IN Public registries Docker Hub, npm, PyPI… + Your CI builds images, jars, charts ↓ everything flows through ↓ ARTIFACTORY · the warehouse Remote (cache) Local (your builds) Virtual (one URL) Federated (multi-site) XRAY · the inspector deep recursive scan → CVEs · licenses · SBOM → Policy + Watch → violation ↓ only what passes the gate ↓ OUT Developers & CI resolve via one virtual URL → Production approved artifacts only If you remember 4 things: Artifactory = the single warehouse for every binary, in and out. 4 repo types : Local (yours), Remote (cached internet), Virtual (one URL over both), Federated (multi-site). Xray = the inspector doing deep recursive scans for CVEs & license risk, continuously. Policy (the rule) + Watch (the scope) = how findings turn into enforced violations. ← Back to Home Next → Replication & Federation All facts link to primary JFrog documentation, current as of June 2026. Product names belong to JFrog Ltd. esc ↑ ↓ navigate ↵ open / or ⌘K to search

no native type fits. xray what xray actually is jfrog xray is a software composition analysis (sca) tool. it scans everything artifactory stores — packages, container images, builds — to detect security vulnerabilities, malicious packages, license risks, and operational issues. it's natively integrated with artifactory, so binary scanning turns on with a single checkbox. jfrog xray docs . 🔍 one-sentence analogy if artifactory is the warehouse, xray is the security inspector living inside it — x-raying every box that arrives, cross-checking it against a global database of known threats, and raising an alarm (or locking the door) when something dangerous is found. 🛡️ vulnerabilities matches components against cve databases (nvd, github, vendor feeds + jfrog's own security research) to find known flaws. ⚖️ license compliance flags open-source licenses your org bans or must track (e.g. gpl in a proprietary product). 🐛 malicious packages detects deliberately poisoned packages — including malicious ml models on hugging face. 📋 sbom & impact generates a full software bill of materials and shows the impact graph of any flaw across your components. sources: xray architecture overview , jfrog sbom . xray how scanning actually works xray's superpower is deep recursive scanning . it doesn't just look at your top-level package — it peels open every layer: dependencies, dependencies-of-dependencies, files inside docker layers, archives inside archives. jfrog impact analysis . deep recursive scan — peeling the onion your docker image base os layer app package (npm/jar) direct dependency ↳ vulnerable lib · cve-2021-44228 xray finds the vulnerable component buried 5 levels deep, then uses impact analysis to show every artifact, build, and image affected by it. jfrog vulnerability detection . the scanning lifecycle index. when a repo is marked for xray, every artifact is broken into its component graph and indexed. scan. each component is matched against the vulnerability + license databases. continuous re-check. when a new cve is published, xray re-evaluates already-stored artifacts — no rescan needed. this is why it catches yesterday's "safe" image today. report & enforce. findings appear as violations (when they break a policy) with severity, affected components, and remediation (which version fixes it). understanding scan results . where you can trigger a scan how best for inside artifactory (automatic) enable xray indexing on a repo — scans on upload continuous coverage of everything stored ci/cd pipeline jf build-scan / jf scan failing a build before bad code ships local machine (shift-left) jf scan on source, jf docker scan on an image catching issues before you even commit ide / pull request jfrog ide plugins & frogbot instant developer feedback + autofix prs source: jfrog cli command summaries . for pr scanning and ide setup, see frogbot & ide integration . xray policies & watches (how enforcement works) finding problems is useless unless you act on them. xray's enforcement runs on two linked objects. this trips up newcomers, so here's the clean split: defines the rules policy a set of rules describing what counts as a problem and what to do about it. type: security · license · operational risk rule: e.g. "severity ≥ high" or "license = gpl" action: notify · fail build · block download · create ticket applied via defines the scope watch a binding that says where a policy applies and turns it on. targets: specific repos, builds, or projects attaches: one or more policies result: matches → violations get generated remember it this way policy = the law (what's illegal + the punishment). watch = the jurisdiction + the police (where the law applies and who enforces it). a finding only becomes a violation when a watch points a policy at the repo where it lives. jfrog policy & governance . severity levels you'll triage critical high medium low unknown a common starter policy: "generate violations for high + critical security issues, but only when a fixed version is available" — so you're not alerted on problems you can't yet fix. stack overflow on fix-version rule . hands-on choose your hands-on track reading gets you understanding; doing gets you skill. pick a track based on how deep you want to go. all three reach the same goal: store an artifact, then scan it with xray. ☁️ jfrog cloud (free tier) 🐳 local docker (self-hosted) ⌨️ concepts + cli fastest path to a working platform. jfrog runs a free cloud tier (aws/azure/gcp) — no install, ready in minutes. best first stop. start free . 1 sign up for the free cloud subscription. go to jfrog.com/start-free → choose cloud (free). you get a tenant like yourname.jfrog.io with artifactory + xray. free tier: ~2 gb storage, 10 gb transfer. free tier announcement . 2 log in to the web ui. explore the two main tabs: application (browse repos/artifacts) and administration (create repos, configure xray). 3 create your first repositories. administration → repositories → + add repository . make a local docker repo ( docker-local ), a remote proxy of docker hub ( docker-remote ), and a virtual repo combining both ( docker ). repo creation walkthrough . 4 connect the cli. install jfrog cli (see the cli tab) then run jf c add and paste your *.jfrog.io url + a token. verify with jf rt ping . 5 push an image. jf docker push yourname.jfrog.io/docker-local/demo:1.0 — then find it in the ui under your local repo. 6 turn on xray. administration → xray → indexed resources → add docker-local . the image scans automatically. 7 create a policy + watch. administration → xray → watches & policies . make a security policy (rule: severity ≥ high, action: notify) and a watch targeting docker-local . policies/watches video . 8 read the results. open the artifact → xray tab. review vulnerabilities by severity, affected components, and fix versions. you've completed the loop. 🎉 why start here zero infrastructure to maintain, real cloud ui, and everything in this guide works out of the box. graduate to local docker once you want to understand the moving parts. run the whole platform on your mac (orbstack/docker). you'll see exactly what services make up artifactory + xray. requires docker engine 25+ & docker compose v2; arm64 (apple silicon) is supported via docker. install guide . heads up on resources the full platform (artifactory + xray + postgresql + rabbitmq) wants ~4 cpu / 8 gb ram. your 8 gb mac setup is the realistic minimum — close other heavy apps. 1 download the trial docker-compose bundle. curl -l "https://releases.jfrog.io/artifactory/jfrog-prox/org/artifactory/pro/docker/jfrog-platform-trial-prox/[release]/jfrog-platform-trial-prox-[release]-compose.tar.gz" -o jfrog-compose.tar.gz source 2 extract & enter the directory. tar -xvf jfrog-compose.tar.gz cd jfrog-platform-trial-prox-* 3 run the config script (sets up folder structure + the .env file compose uses). follow its prompts. 4 start everything. docker compose -p trial-pro up -d give it a few minutes — xray boots after artifactory and postgresql are healthy. xray install steps . 5 open the ui. browse to http://localhost:8082 . default login admin / password — change it immediately. 6 apply your free license. paste the license key from your jfrog account to unlock xray. 7 from here, follow cloud steps 3–8 — create local/remote/virtual repos, push an image, enable xray indexing, set a policy + watch, read results. the ui is identical. 8 tear down cleanly. docker compose -p trial-pro down add -v only if you want to wipe the data volumes too. prefer kubernetes? see kubernetes / helm setup for installing artifactory on a cluster with the jfrog helm charts. for when you don't want to run a server — just learn the commands & integration patterns. the jfrog cli binary is jf . one tool drives artifactory and xray. cli summaries . 1 install the cli (macos, homebrew): brew install jfrog-cli or one-liner: curl -fl https://install-cli.jfrog.io | sh . verify: jf --version . 2 configure a server connection. jf c add my-jpd --url=https://yourname.jfrog.io --interactive use an access token, not a password. test: jf rt ping . 3 scan your source code (shift-left) — no upload needed: jf scan . scans the dependencies in the current project against xray. source-scan docs . 4 scan a local docker image: jf docker scan my-app:1.0.0 first run downloads ~100 mb of xray scanner binaries. jf docker docs . 5 scan an artifact already in artifactory, against a watch: jf xr scan --target "docker-local/demo:1.0" --watches "my-watch" xray cli commands . 6 wire it into github actions (fail the build on critical issues): - name: security scan run: jf build-scan ${{ github.repository }} ${{ github.run_number }} --fail=true --vuln=critical ci integration . note: the build must be added under administration → xray → indexed resources first. mental model for the cli jf rt … = artifactory (uploads, downloads, repos). jf xr … = xray (scans). jf scan / jf docker scan / jf build-scan = convenient shortcuts that combine both. hands-on cli cheat sheet the commands you'll reach for most. jf is the binary; older docs use jfrog . reference . command what it does jf c add add a jfrog server connection jf rt ping test the artifactory connection jf rt u <file> <repo> upload (deploy) an artifact jf rt dl <repo/path> download an artifact jf docker push <image> push a docker image (auto-login + build-info) jf scan . scan local source dependencies with xray jf docker scan <image> scan a local docker image jf xr scan --target … --watches … scan an artifact in artifactory against a watch jf build-scan <name> <num> --fail=true scan a build in ci; fail on violations synthesis the full mental model on one screen everything above, compressed. if you can re-draw this from memory, you understand the platform. in public registries docker hub, npm, pypi… + your ci builds images, jars, charts ↓ everything flows through ↓ artifactory · the warehouse remote (cache) local (your builds) virtual (one url) federated (multi-site) xray · the inspector deep recursive scan → cves · licenses · sbom → policy + watch → violation ↓ only what passes the gate ↓ out developers & ci resolve via one virtual url → production approved artifacts only if you remember 4 things: artifactory = the single warehouse for every binary, in and out. 4 repo types : local (yours), remote (cached internet), virtual (one url over both), federated (multi-site). xray = the inspector doing deep recursive scans for cves & license risk, continuously. policy (the rule) + watch (the scope) = how findings turn into enforced violations. ← back to home next → replication & federation all facts title: Kubernetes & Helm setup
body: Skip to content On this page Two distinct goals Install Artifactory on K8s Master & join keys Artifactory as Helm registry OCI push & pull Home / Kubernetes / Helm setup Artifactory Kubernetes How-to Kubernetes & Helm setup There are two distinct things people mean by “JFrog and Kubernetes.” One: run Artifactory itself on a cluster using the official Helm charts. Two: use Artifactory as the Helm/OCI registry your charts live in. This page covers both, in order. Orientation Two distinct goals — don't conflate them A Run Artifactory on K8s Deploy the JFrog Platform itself into a Kubernetes cluster using the official Helm charts. This is an infrastructure/install task. B Use Artifactory as a Helm registry Store and serve your own Helm charts (classic or OCI) from an Artifactory repository. This is a day-to-day usage task. Both rely on Helm, which is why they get confused. Sources: Install Artifactory on Kubernetes , Helm Chart Repositories . Goal A Installing Artifactory on Kubernetes with Helm JFrog publishes official Helm charts. You add the chart repo, prepare a couple of secrets (master + join keys), then install into a namespace. JFrog — Install Artifactory on Kubernetes . 1) Add the JFrog Helm chart repository helm repo add jfrog https://charts.jfrog.io helm repo update 2) Install Artifactory into its own namespace helm upgrade --install artifactory \ --set artifactory.masterKey=${MASTER_KEY} \ --set artifactory.joinKey=${JOIN_KEY} \ --namespace artifactory --create-namespace \ jfrog/artifactory upgrade --install is idempotent — the same command installs the first time and upgrades thereafter, which fits a GitOps workflow. For production, supply a values.yaml (external database, ingress, persistent volumes) instead of bare --set flags. Prereq Master & join keys — generate them first JFrog microservices authenticate to each other with a join key , and Artifactory encrypts config with a master key . You generate both as random hex and pass them to the chart (ideally as Kubernetes secrets, not inline). Generate 32-byte hex keys export MASTER_KEY=$(openssl rand -hex 32) export JOIN_KEY=$(openssl rand -hex 32) # better: store them as a secret rather than --set on the CLI kubectl create namespace artifactory kubectl create secret generic my-masterkey \ --from-literal=master-key=${MASTER_KEY} -n artifactory kubectl create secret generic my-joinkey \ --from-literal=join-key=${JOIN_KEY} -n artifactory Why these matter Lose the master key and you can't decrypt your stored configuration; mismatch the join key across nodes and the microservices won't trust each other. Generate once, store securely, reuse on upgrades. Goal B Using Artifactory as a Helm chart registry Artifactory can host Helm charts in a local repo, proxy public charts via a remote repo, and aggregate both behind a virtual repo. You then point the Helm client at the virtual repo's API URL. JFrog — Helm Chart Repositories . Add your Artifactory Helm repo to the Helm client helm repo add my-charts \ https://yourname.jfrog.io/artifactory/api/helm/helm-virtual \ --username <user> --password <token> helm repo update L helm-local your charts + R helm-remote proxies public charts → both behind → V helm-virtual single URL clients use Clients only ever talk to the virtual repo; Artifactory resolves from local-first then remote. JFrog — Helm Chart Repositories . Modern OCI: push & pull charts as OCI artifacts Modern Helm (v3.8+) treats charts as OCI artifacts — the same registry mechanism container images use. Artifactory supports this directly, so you can push and pull charts to an OCI-enabled repository. JFrog — Helm OCI Repositories . Package, push, and pull a chart over OCI # package your chart into a .tgz helm package ./my-chart # → my-chart-1.0.0.tgz # push it to an OCI repo in Artifactory helm push my-chart-1.0.0.tgz \ oci://yourname.jfrog.io/helm-oci-local # pull it back / install from OCI helm pull oci://yourname.jfrog.io/helm-oci-local/my-chart --version 1.0.0 helm install my-release \ oci://yourname.jfrog.io/helm-oci-local/my-chart --version 1.0.0 Authenticate first Run helm registry login yourname.jfrog.io -u <user> -p <token> before pushing over OCI. The token is one you mint on the Access tokens page, scoped to deploy on the chart repo. Sources: Install on K8s , Helm Chart Repositories , Helm OCI Repositories . ← Previous Access tokens & permissions Back to → Home All facts link to primary JFrog documentation, current as of June 2026. Product names belong to JFrog Ltd. esc ↑ ↓ navigate ↵ open / or ⌘K to search

title: JFrog Pipelines
body: Skip to content On this page What Pipelines is Core building blocks The YAML DSL First pipeline (steps) Hello World example Home / JFrog Pipelines CI/CD Platform How-to JFrog Pipelines Pipelines is the platform's native CI/CD engine. You declare resources and steps in a YAML DSL stored in your Git repo, connect it through integrations, and Pipelines runs it on build nodes — with Artifactory and Xray built in. Concept What JFrog Pipelines is JFrog Pipelines is a CI/CD automation engine built into the JFrog Platform. You define pipelines in a YAML-based Pipelines DSL — a set of key-value files known as a pipeline config — and store them in a Git-compatible source repository. Pipelines syncs from that source, loads your DSL, and runs continuous integration and delivery on build nodes. JFrog — Creating Pipelines . How is this different from GitHub Actions? Both run YAML-defined CI/CD. Pipelines' edge is native integration with Artifactory (build-info, promotion) and Xray (scanning gates) plus centrally-managed, secure integrations and reusable resources. If you already live in the JFrog platform, Pipelines keeps everything in one place. You can also combine it with Jenkins, GitHub Actions, or Azure DevOps. Building blocks The core building blocks Concept What it is Integration A stored, secure connection to an external service — GitHub, Artifactory, Kubernetes, etc. You provide the URL endpoint and credentials once; Pipelines stores them centrally. Pipeline source The Git repo (added via an integration) that holds your YAML DSL. Pipelines auto-syncs whenever you commit a change to the DSL. Resource A source or destination of data used by the pipeline — e.g. a GitRepo , a BuildInfo , an image. Resources connect steps and can trigger them. Step A unit of execution (e.g. a Bash step). Steps declare inputResources and inputSteps to express dependencies and ordering. Pipeline The named collection of steps and resources that together form a workflow. Sources: JFrog — Pipeline Hello World , Pipelines step-by-step . DSL The YAML DSL: resources + steps A pipeline config has two main sections. Resources declare the data sources/destinations; pipelines declare the steps that act on them. Steps wire together via inputSteps (run after) and inputResources (consume/produce). JFrog — Workflow examples . A step that runs after another step succeeds pipelines: - name: demo_pipeline steps: - name: build_step type: Bash configuration: inputResources: - name: my_git_repo # a GitRepo resource execution: onExecute: - echo "building…" - name: publish_step type: Bash configuration: inputSteps: - name: build_step # runs only after build_step execution: onExecute: - echo "publishing build-info to Artifactory" How-to Standing up your first pipeline Sign in to the JFrog Platform with your Artifactory credentials. On JFrog Cloud, a default dynamic node pool is provisioned, so you already have build nodes. Add a GitHub integration. Administration → Pipelines → Integrations → add a GitHub integration with a personal access token that has repo + admin OAuth scopes. Add a pipeline source. Point Pipelines at the repo (and branch) holding your YAML DSL, connected through that integration. Pipelines syncs and processes the DSL. Commit DSL changes. Every commit to the YAML re-syncs the source and reloads the pipeline. Run it. Trigger manually or via a GitRepo resource that fires steps when the repo changes. Source: JFrog — Pipeline Example: Hello World . Worked example The "Hello World" example, end to end 1 Fork example repo jfrog-pipelines-simple-example → 2 Add GitHub integration token w/ repo+admin scope → 3 Add pipeline source points at the YAML → 4 Sync & run GitRepo trigger fires steps The official Hello World demonstrates a GitHub integration, a pipeline source, a GitRepo trigger, and inputResources / inputSteps dependencies. Fork the JFrog example repo, update the path values, commit, and watch it run. JFrog — Hello World . Where Artifactory & Xray plug in Add a BuildInfo resource to publish build-info to Artifactory, then a promotion step (see Build promotion ) gated by an Xray scan. That turns a plain CI pipeline into a secured, traceable delivery pipeline — the whole point of doing CI inside JFrog. ← Previous Frogbot & IDE Next → Access tokens & permissions All facts link to primary JFrog documentation, current as of June 2026. title: Release Bundles & Distribution
body: Skip to content On this page The problem What a Release Bundle is Bundle lifecycle Distribution & edge nodes Create & distribute (CLI) Bundle vs. promotion Home / Release Bundles & Distribution Artifactory Supply chain Deep-dive Release Bundles & Distribution A Release Bundle is a sealed, signed manifest of exactly which artifacts make up a release. JFrog Distribution then ships that bundle out to edge nodes and remote sites — so what arrives is provably identical to what you signed. Concept The problem Release Bundles solve Promotion moves binaries inside one platform. But shipping a release out — to a customer site, an edge location, or an air-gapped network — raises new questions: which exact files belong to "v2.4.0"? Were any swapped in transit? Can the receiving side prove provenance? A loose folder of artifacts answers none of these. A Release Bundle is the answer: an immutable, signed specification of the precise set of artifacts that constitute a release, with a checksum-verified manifest. JFrog — Release Lifecycle Management . Definition What's inside a Release Bundle A curated artifact set. You select artifacts (by AQL query, build, or path) that make up the release. The bundle records each one's checksum. Immutability. Once created, the bundle's contents can't change. A new release means a new bundle version. Cryptographic signing. The bundle manifest is signed with a GPG key, so any recipient can verify it wasn't tampered with. Provenance metadata. The bundle carries the source build-info and (optionally) Xray scan results, giving the receiver evidence of what was vetted. 📜 One-sentence analogy A Release Bundle is a tamper-evident shipping manifest with a wax seal . It lists every item in the crate, and the seal proves nobody opened it between the warehouse and the customer's loading dock. Flow The bundle lifecycle 1 Create select artifacts → sign → 2 Sign GPG-signed manifest → 3 Distribute push to edge nodes → 4 Verify & consume checksum-checked on arrival The bundle is created and sealed once, then distributed to one or many targets. Each target verifies signatures and checksums before exposing the artifacts. JFrog — Distributing Release Bundles . Distribution Distribution & edge nodes JFrog Distribution is the service that takes a signed Release Bundle and delivers it to edge nodes — lightweight Artifactory instances positioned close to consumers (a factory floor, a CDN POP, an air-gapped enclave). Edge nodes are read-only consumption points: they receive distributed bundles but aren't general-purpose repositories. JFrog Distribution docs . Component Role Distribution service Orchestrates creating, signing, and shipping bundles to targets. Edge node A consumption-only Artifactory at the destination that hosts distributed bundles. Distribution target The rule selecting which edge node(s) a given bundle goes to. Why edge nodes matter Consumers at the edge pull from a nearby node — fast and resilient — while you keep a single signed source of truth centrally. Air-gapped sites receive the bundle as a verifiable package rather than opening a live connection to your main platform. CLI Create and distribute a bundle (CLI) The JFrog CLI exposes release-bundle commands under jf rbc / jf rbu (create/update) and jf rbd (distribute), depending on the lifecycle version. The conceptual flow: JFrog CLI — Release Bundles . Create a signed bundle from a spec, then distribute # 1. Create release bundle v2.4.0 from an artifact spec jf release-bundle-create my-app 2.4.0 \ --spec=release-spec.json --signing-key=my-gpg-key # 2. Distribute it to the configured edge nodes jf release-bundle-distribute my-app 2.4.0 \ --site="edge-eu-*" The spec selects artifacts (by AQL/path); the signing key seals the manifest; the distribute step fans it out to matching edge sites. Exact subcommands vary by Release Lifecycle Management version — check the linked CLI reference for your platform. Compare Release Bundle vs. build promotion Aspect Build promotion Release Bundle + Distribution Scope Within one platform Across platforms / out to edges Mutability Status changes over time Immutable once signed Integrity proof Checksum tracking GPG-signed manifest Primary goal Advance maturity stage Ship a sealed release safely Air-gap friendly No Yes They compose: promote a build to release-local with an Xray gate (see Build promotion ), then bundle and distribute that vetted set to the edge. ← Previous Build promotion Next → Artifactory REST API All facts link to primary JFrog documentation, current as of June 2026. title: Replication & Federation
body: Skip to content On this page Why sync at all Replication Push vs. pull Federated repositories Which to choose How to set it up Home / Replication & Federation Artifactory Multi-site Deep-dive Replication & Federation Once you run JFrog in more than one location, you need copies of your artifacts to stay in sync. JFrog gives you two mechanisms — classic replication and federated repositories. They solve overlapping problems differently. Concept Why sync artifacts across sites at all? A single Artifactory instance is one point in the world. The moment you have teams in London and Tokyo, a CI farm in another region, or a disaster-recovery site, builds in one place need the same binaries that live in another. Pulling everything across an ocean on every build is slow and fragile. 01 Latency Developers and CI resolve dependencies from a local copy instead of crossing continents on every download. 02 Availability If one site or its network goes down, the others still have their own copy and keep building. 03 Disaster recovery A synced standby instance lets you fail over with the artifacts already in place. JFrog offers two answers: replication (an older, one-directional copy job) and federation (newer, automatic, multi-directional mirroring). JFrog — Repository Replication . Replication Replication — copying repositories between instances Replication synchronizes the contents of a repository on one Artifactory instance with a repository on another. It runs on a schedule (cron) or can be triggered as an event, and it copies artifacts plus their properties. Replication works on local and remote repositories. Source . Key trait: it's one-directional A single replication has a clear source and target. Site A pushes to Site B, or Site B pulls from Site A — but one replication relationship only flows one way. To mirror both directions you configure two separate jobs. Replication Push vs. pull replication Mode Who initiates How it works Typical use Push The source instance The source repo pushes new/changed artifacts out to a target repo on a remote instance. A central build site fanning releases out to regional mirrors. Pull The target instance A remote repo's "replication" pulls content from its upstream Artifactory on a schedule. An edge/DR site keeping itself current from a master. A Source JPD has the artifacts → push → B Target JPD receives a copy — or, reversed initiator — A Source JPD has the artifacts ← pull ← B Target JPD fetches the copy Push and pull move data the same direction (A → B); they differ only in which side starts the transfer . Push is good when the source can reach the target; pull is good when only the target can reach out (e.g. firewalled edge). JFrog Replication . Federation Federated repositories — automatic multi-directional mirroring A Federated repository behaves like a local repo, but it is bi-directionally mirrored across multiple members — up to 10 repositories living on different JFrog Platform Deployments (JPDs). Write to any member and the change propagates to all the others, automatically and asynchronously. JFrog — Federated Repositories . Bi-directional by design. Every member is both source and target — no manual two-way setup. Deploy to London, it appears in Tokyo, and vice-versa. Async, queue-backed. Changes are replicated through a persistent queue, so a temporary network blip doesn't lose data — it catches up when connectivity returns. Configuration is synced too. Repository configuration (not just artifacts) is kept aligned across members. Up to 10 members. A federation can span up to ten member repositories across JPDs. Enterprise tier. Federated repositories require an Enterprise X (or higher) subscription. The mental shortcut Replication = a copy job you schedule, one direction at a time. Federation = a self-healing cluster of mirror repos that stay in sync automatically, both ways. Federation is the modern default for active-active multi-site; replication still fits one-way DR and edge-cache patterns. Decision Which one should you use? Dimension Replication Federated repos Direction One-way per job Multi-directional (all members) Trigger Scheduled / event Automatic on every write Members Two endpoints per job Up to 10 across JPDs Config sync No (artifacts & properties) Yes (config too) Resilience Re-runs on schedule Persistent queue, self-healing License Pro / Enterprise Enterprise X+ Best for DR standby, edge caches, one-way distribution Active-active global teams, single source of truth Sources: JFrog Replication , JFrog Federated Repositories . How-to Setting each one up Configure replication (UI) Open the repo. Administration → Repositories → select a local or remote repo → Replications tab. Add a replication. Provide the target instance URL, the target repository key, and credentials (use an access token). Pick a schedule. Set a cron expression (e.g. every hour) and choose whether to enable event-based replication for near-real-time push. Save & test. Run it once manually and confirm artifacts land in the target. Create a federated repository (UI) Add repository → Federated. Choose the package type, then create the repo on your first JPD. Add members. In the Federated configuration, add member URLs for the other JPDs (the platform must be aware of those JPDs via Mission Control / circle-of-trust). Deploy anywhere. Push an artifact to any member; verify it appears on the others within seconds. Tip for your labs On the free single-instance tier you can't truly demonstrate multi-JPD federation — you need at least two deployments. You can still create the repo types and read the configuration screens to understand the model. For a real two-site test, stand up two self-hosted instances (see the Docker lab on the Fundamentals page). ← Previous Fundamentals Next → Build promotion All facts link to primary JFrog documentation, current as of June 2026. title: The Artifactory REST API
body: Skip to content On this page Authentication Key endpoints Calling with curl The jf api shortcut Create a repo via API Best practices Home / Artifactory REST API Artifactory Integration Reference The Artifactory REST API Everything you can click in the UI, you can automate over HTTP. This page covers how to authenticate, the endpoints you'll actually use, and how to call them from curl and the JFrog CLI's built-in API shortcut. Foundation Authentication — three ways in Every Artifactory REST call needs to identify you. There are three accepted methods; pick access tokens for automation. JFrog — Intro to the REST APIs . Method How it's sent Use when Access token (recommended) Authorization: Bearer <token> Any automation — scoped, revocable, no password exposure. Basic auth -u user:password Quick local tests only; avoid in scripts. API key X-JFrog-Art-Api: <key> Legacy integrations (being phased out in favor of tokens). See the Access tokens & permissions page for how to mint and scope a token. Reference The endpoints you'll actually use Endpoint Method Does /artifactory/api/system/ping GET Health check — returns OK . /artifactory/api/repositories GET List all repositories. /artifactory/api/repositories/<key> PUT Create/configure a repository. /artifactory/api/repositories/<key> GET Read one repo's config. /artifactory/api/storage/<repo>/<path> GET File/folder info & metadata. /artifactory/api/build/promote/<name>/<num> POST Promote a build (see Build promotion). /access/api/v1/users/<username> GET/PUT Manage a user. /access/api/v1/groups/<group> GET/PUT Manage a group. /access/api/v1/tokens POST Create an access token. Note the two base paths: /artifactory/api/... for repository/storage operations, and /access/api/... for identity (users, groups, tokens). Sources: JFrog REST APIs , REST quickstart . How-to Calling the API with curl Health check curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $JF_TOKEN" \ https://yourname.jfrog.io/artifactory/api/system/ping # → OK List every repository as JSON curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $JF_TOKEN" \ https://yourname.jfrog.io/artifactory/api/repositories | jq . Put the token in an env var Never paste tokens inline in shared scripts. Export JF_TOKEN once (ideally from a secret manager) and reference it. Rotate it on a schedule. Shortcut The jf api shortcut If you've already configured a server with jf c add , the CLI can call any Artifactory REST endpoint using your stored credentials — no manual auth header needed. JFrog — Use API endpoints via CLI . Same "list repositories" call, authenticated automatically jf rt curl -XGET /api/repositories # or the generic platform call: jf api /artifactory/api/repositories This is the fastest way to script against Artifactory during your labs: configure once, then drive any endpoint without juggling tokens in every command. Worked example Create a local Docker repo via the API PUT a repository configuration curl -XPUT -H "Authorization: Bearer $JF_TOKEN" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ https://yourname.jfrog.io/artifactory/api/repositories/docker-local \ -d '{ "rclass": "local", "packageType": "docker" }' Swap "rclass": "remote" with a "url" field for a proxy repo, or "virtual" with a "repositories" list to aggregate. This is exactly what the UI's "Add Repository" form does under the hood. Guidance Best practices Use access tokens, never passwords in automation — they're scoped and revocable. Prefer jf api / jf rt curl in CLI contexts so credentials stay in CLI config, not your shell history. Check /system/ping first in scripts to fail fast on connectivity/auth problems. Treat config-as-code. Keep repo-creation JSON in version control so your platform is reproducible. Rotate tokens and scope them to the minimum permission target needed (see Access tokens ). ← Previous Release Bundles & Distribution Next → Frogbot & IDE All facts link to primary JFrog documentation, current as of June 2026.
